pattern: Adding or subtracting powers of two to reach zero is the classic signed binary representation problem: a run of consecutive one-bits is always cheaper to clear with one carrying addition than with one subtraction per bit.
hints: Working through the number bit by bit from the lowest bit, ask: is it cheaper to clear a 1-bit by subtracting it, or by adding just enough to carry it into the next bit?

A single isolated 1-bit should just be subtracted. But a run of two or more consecutive 1-bits is cheaper to handle by adding one small power of two, which carries through the whole run and turns it into a single higher bit.

Scan the bits from lowest to highest with a small carry flag: whenever you see a 1-bit, decide between the add-and-carry and the subtract-and-clear cases by checking whether the next bit is also set, counting one operation each time you act.

edge_inputs.description: a power of two greater than one
a long run of consecutive one-bits just under a power of two
a run of three consecutive one-bits collapses to two operations, not three
two isolated one-bits should not be merged
scaling by two (an extra trailing zero bit) must not change the answer

stress.description: uniformly random values across the full range
numbers built from long runs of consecutive one-bits, optionally shifted
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(1, 16)
    val = (1 << k) - 1
    shift = rng.randint(0, 16 - k)
    val = val << shift
    val = max(1, min(100000, val))
    return [val]
